text.title: One Person Rollback Runbook Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every application of `One Person Rollback Runbook` must enforce. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: The runbook MUST cover all three layers: app code, DB migrations, feature flags. Missing any layer = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Rollbacks that only revert code leave broken migrations or stale flags; partial rollback is worse than no rollback because it hides the real state.

rule.statement: Every step MUST carry a `verify_cmd` (shell-runnable) and `expected` (string contained in stdout). Steps without verify are rejected.
rule.rationale: An unverifiable step is a hope; under pressure the operator skips it. Verifiable steps are auditable and survive panic-mode.

rule.statement: The runbook names the runtime owner (operator handle + role + escalation contact). No 'team' or 'we' as owner.
rule.rationale: Anonymous runbooks rot. A named runtime owner is the only auditable backstop when the on-call alert fires.

rule.statement: The runbook MUST be dry-run end-to-end weekly against staging. Unrehearsed runbooks (last_dry_run > 14d) MUST be flagged as `needs-dry-run` before use.
rule.rationale: A runbook only works if the operator has hit the commands in muscle memory. Weekly drills are the cost of single-operator reliability.

rule.statement: After each rollback, the smoke-check suite MUST be executed and pass before declaring 'rolled back'. Skipping smoke = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Rolling back to a green tag does not guarantee green prod — DNS, CDN cache, and config drift can keep it red. Smoke is the only honest signal.

rule.statement: When preconditions in `Applies If` fail OR any item in `Skip If` triggers, do not run this methodology. Document the skip rationale in the artefact's parent log.
rule.rationale: Misapplied methodologies are worse than no methodology — they consume cycles and produce false confidence.
